question: 정강이뼈 융기(무릎 아래 튀어나온 부분)가 뭐야?
ground_truth: The tibial tuberosity is a spot on your knee where tape is put during the taping steps for knee pain. The tape starts or finishes at the tibial tuberosity to help with knee pain.
contexts:
##  General Knee Pain / Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome

Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) is a painful condition that can relate to a type of mal-tracking of the kneecap (patella) (see Fig. 3.1). There are many causes for this condition, such as an over-pronation of the subtalar joint (STJ) of the ankle and poor foot biomechanics. Weakness of the inner quadriceps muscle (called the vastus medialis) can also contribute to PFPS, especially the vastus medialis oblique (VMO) fibers, which are thought to atrophy due to pain and minimal swelling. In addition, weak gluteus medius (Gmed) and gluteus maximus (Gmax) can cause this type of knee pain. The knee joint is therefore what I refer to as “a weak link in the kinetic chain,” and typically the presentation of the pain is not where the problem lies.

> **Figure 3.1**: General knee pain/PFPS.

###  Taping Procedure: Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Attach a “Y” strip from the superior aspect of the patella and apply the tape to the medial and lateral sides of the patella, finishing by crossing over the tibial tuberosity | Long sitting, knee at 90° flexion | 0% |
| 2 | Apply a “Y” strip from the tibial tuberosity, lay the tape medially and laterally around the patella to overlap the first “Y” application, finishing near the quadriceps tendon | Long sitting, knee at 90° flexion | Little to no stretch |
| 3 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |
| 4 | Lower the limb back down to the couch and observe the “wrinkling” of the tape | — | — |

> **Figure 3.2**: First application of the “Y” strip starting from the superior aspect of the patella and finishing at the tibial tuberosity.

> **Figure 3.3**: Second application of the “Y” strip starting from the tibial tuberosity and finishing at the starting point of the first application.

Knee pain (patellofemoral pain syndrome)

Chen et al. (2008) conducted a study of the “Biomechanics effects of Kinesio Taping for persons with patellofemoral pain syndrome during stair climbing.” They concluded that this type of kinesiology taping could reduce pain and improve the ratio of VMO/vastus lateralis (VL) for the mechanism of patellar stability during stair climbing.

In general, PFPS is possibly one of the most common conditions that I have treated with some form of strapping and taping. This includes conventional athletic taping methods using zinc oxide (Z/O), as with the McConnell technique to control patellar alignment, as well as kinesiology taping. I like and use all the varieties of taping techniques as each of them has a unique effect upon the knee. However, my choice at the present time for helping to reduce athletes’ and patients’ persistent knee pains is the kinesiology taping method.

---

##

question: 키네시오 테이핑이 무릎 아픈 데 도움이 될까?
question_en: Can Kinesio Taping help my knee pain?
ground_truth: Kinesio Taping can help reduce knee pain and improve how your kneecap moves, especially when going up stairs.
contexts:

question: 그림 3.13이 뭘 보여줘?
ground_truth: Figure 3.13 shows how my knee should look after the tape is on.
contexts:
Knee Malalignment Taping Technique

The two techniques demonstrated below are potentially very simple in one respect because only one piece of tape is required. I do find in practice that sometimes the simple approach can work very well. Just remember that the majority of knee pain is merely a symptom, and the causative factor for the patient’s pain might be in another structure, such as the hip joint, or possibly over-pronation of the foot.

The application of a single piece of tape can be done on its own with a 2-inch small “I” strip. The technique can also be applied in combination with the full knee taping, as described below. Another option is to use the 4-inch (Big Daddy) piece of tape, as shown in Figure 3.9, in the alternative technique below.

### Taping Procedure: Knee Malalignment (Standard)

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Apply a smaller “I” strip from the lateral side of the knee (LCL), apply a medial glide to the lateral side of the patella, increase tension to 50–75%, and apply over the patella to maintain the gliding motion | Long sitting, knee straight | 50–75% |
| 2 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |

> **Figure 3.8**: a: Apply the tape (with tension) from the lateral side and finish medially, whilst applying pressure to the patella from the thumb. b: Finished position.

> **Figure 3.9**: The 4-inch tape (Big Daddy) has been specifically designed for this taping application.

Patellofemoral syndrome (knee pain)

### Taping Procedure: Knee Malalignment (Alternative Technique)

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Apply the tape from the lateral aspect of the patella, near the lateral collateral ligament (LCL) | Long sitting, knee straight | — |
| 2 | With the thumb, apply a medial glide to the lateral side of the patella, increase tension to the superior part of the tape to 50–75% and apply over the patella | Long sitting, knee straight | 50–75% (superior part) |
| 3 | With the thumb, apply a medial glide to the lateral side of the patella, increase tension to the inferior part of the tape to 50–75% and apply over the patella | Long sitting, knee straight | 50–75% (inferior part) |
| 4 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |

> **Figure 3.10**: First application of the tape, from the lateral side of the knee.

> **Figure 3.11**: Tension the superior part of the tape with medial glide from the thumb.

> **Figure 3.12**: Apply tension to the inferior part of the tape with medial glide from the thumb.

Once you have applied the tape then the knee should look like Figure 3.13.

> **Figure 3.13**: How it should look after the tape has been applied.

---

##

question: 무릎 아플 때 테이핑 어떻게 해?
question_en: How can I tape my knee for pain?
ground_truth: You can use one piece of tape for your knee. Sit with your knee straight, and apply a small strip from the outside of your knee. Use your thumb to push the kneecap inward while sticking the tape over it with some tension. Heat the tape to make it stick. You can also use a bigger piece of tape and do the same steps, adding tension to the top and bottom parts of the tape as you press the kneecap inward. When you finish, your knee should look like the pictures in the instructions.

question: 무릎 안쪽이 아픈데, 어떡하지?
question_en: My MCL hurts, what do I do?
ground_truth: Put tape across the inside of your knee, then add two small strips over the sore spot to make an X. Keep your knee bent. Warm up the tape glue.
contexts:
Lateral Knee Pain: Iliotibial Band Friction Syndrome / Lateral Meniscus / Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL)

I personally believe that everyone who enjoys running, whether competitively or recreationally, will, at some stage in their life, experience one of these conditions to the lateral side of their knee to varying degrees. There are many reasons why an athlete or patient presents with lateral knee pain and the most common reason is iliotibial band friction syndrome (ITBFS). The reason why the patient is experiencing lateral knee pain could simply be from wearing incorrect footwear, especially among those who are new to running.

It is not within the scope of this book to discuss all causes of lateral knee pain (Fig. 3.14); however, the following kinesiology taping technique will help reduce the patient’s presenting pain. Through this fantastic technique, it is possible to provide enough pain relief to enable the patient/athlete to continue participating in the sport they love, as well as taking part in a physical therapy assessment (to identify the underlying cause(s)) and subsequent treatment.

> **Figure 3.14**: ITBFS/lateral meniscus and the LCL. Iliotibial band friction syndrome. Gerdy’s tubercle. Lateral collateral ligament (LCL). Lateral meniscus.

### Taping Procedure: Lateral Knee Pain

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Apply an “I” strip from the Gerdy’s tubercle (insertion of IT band), cross the lateral femoral condyle, and finish along the length of the IT band | Side lying, knee flexed, hip flexed and adducted | Little to no stretch |
| 2 | Using one small “I” strip, apply across the area of pain to the lateral femoral condyle | Same as above | 75–100% |
| 3 | Using another small “I” strip, place across the area of the other “I” strip to form an “X” shape across the IT band on the lateral femoral condyle | Same as above | 75–100% |
| 4 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |

> **Figure 3.15**: First application starting from Gerdy’s tubercle and finishing along the IT band.

> **Figure 3.16**: Second application of the tape using one small “I” strip across the area of pain.

> **Figure 3.17**: Third application of the tape using a small “I” strip to form an “X” shape across the IT band on the lateral femoral condyle.

Runner’s knee/iliotibial band friction syndrome

---

## Medial Knee Pain: Medial Collateral Ligament / Medial Meniscus

Medial collateral ligament (MCL) sprains and tears, as well as injuries to the medial meniscus, are considered to be some of the most common injuries, especially in sports and activities like football and skiing. The MCL, in particular, restricts the motion of a movement called “valgus.” This means the knee is restricted from moving inwards, so any excessive force in this plane will often result in the ligament becoming stretched and subsequently torn (or sprained) as well as a potential tear to the medial meniscus (Fig. 3.18).

> **Figure 3.18**: MCL sprain/medial meniscus tear.

### Taping Procedure: Medial Knee Pain

| Step | Application Path | Position | Stretch % |
|------|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Apply an “I” strip across the medial aspect of the knee, starting from the distal inferior attachment onto the tibia and finishing on the proximal attachment to the femur | Knee at 90° flexion | Little to no stretch |
| 2 | Using one small “I” strip, apply across the area of pain | Same as above | 75–100% |
| 3 | Using another small “I” strip, place across the area of the other “I” strip to form an “X” shape across the MCL | Same as above | 75–100% |
| 4 | Heat activate the glue | — | — |

> **Figure 3.19**: First application to the MCL.

> **Figure 3.20**: Second application of the tape using one small “I” strip across the area of pain.

> **Figure 3.21**: Third application of the tape using a small “I” strip to form an “X” shape across the MCL.

Medial knee pain (MCL sprain/medial meniscus)

question: 축구하고 나서 무릎 안쪽이 왜 아프지?
question_en: Why does my inner knee hurt after football?
ground_truth: Hurting on the inside of your knee after football could be from an injury to the medial meniscus or the ligament on that side. These are common injuries in sports like football and skiing. The ligament helps keep your knee from moving inwards, so if you get hit or twist your knee too much, it can stretch or tear, and the meniscus can also tear.
